ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes what it claims is the first practical algorithm for semi-supervised reinforcement learning (S3G), framing a less-studied problem (learning from a mix of labeled and unlabeled MDPs) and using an IRL-style joint reward-and-policy inference that leverages rollouts from an RL policy in labeled MDPs as demonstrations. The approach is clearly differentiated from prior work by combining maximum-entropy trajectory likelihood, importance-sampled partition-function estimation, and a sample-efficient implementation via guided cost learning and MDGPS, which constitutes a non-trivial methodological extension. Empirical results showing gains over both labeled-only and reward-regression baselines (and sometimes even the oracle) further support that the idea and implementation are novel and informative to experts, despite the stated limitation that some labeled MDPs are required.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from problem setup to method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (proposal of S3G and its IRL-style procedure). Notation is consistent and well-defined in the summary (πRL, R̃φ, πθ and the iterative learning loop), and the empirical figures/tables are informative—evaluations across multiple MuJoCo tasks, baselines, and success-rate/sample-complexity metrics support clarity. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough; minor presentation issues (e.g., communicating the practical impact of the importance-sampling variance and labeled/unlabeled distribution assumptions) keep it from being exceptional.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: S3G is evaluated on multiple MuJoCo tasks and shown to outperform a policy trained only on labeled MDPs and a supervised reward-regression baseline, with an oracle comparison included. The algorithmic approach (maximum-entropy trajectory model, importance-sampling estimate of the partition function, and joint reward/policy optimization via guided cost learning and MDGPS) is technically coherent; no obvious mathematical inconsistencies are reported in the summary, though formal proofs are not emphasized. Experimental methodology is reasonable with appropriate baselines and sample-complexity reporting, but the summary does not mention statistical significance tests and the evaluation is limited to simulation under the stated assumption that rewards are shared across MDPs. Given these minor limitations and the acknowledged assumptions (need for some labeled MDPs and possible IS variance), the technical claims appear sound overall.

main_claims: Proposes the first practical algorithm for semi-supervised reinforcement learning (S3G) that leverages unlabeled experience to improve policy generalization beyond policies trained only on labeled MDPs.

Uses an inverse-reinforcement-learning-style procedure (learning a reward and policy jointly) that takes rollouts from an RL policy in labeled MDPs as demonstrations, enabling autonomous reward inference in unlabeled MDPs.

Demonstrates empirically on simulated MuJoCo control tasks that S3G outperforms (a) a policy trained only in labeled MDPs and (b) a policy trained in unlabeled MDPs using a reward function learned by supervised regression, and in some cases yields better performance than an oracle due to improved reward shaping.
Presents a sample-efficient implementation using guided cost learning and mirror-descent guided policy search (MDGPS), and provides code for reproducing simulated experiments.
_summary.methodology: Train an entropy-regularized RL policy πRL in labeled MDPs, collect its rollouts DπRL, then iteratively in unlabeled MDPs alternate between (a) learning a parametric reward R̃φ by maximizing likelihood of DπRL under a maximum-entropy trajectory model (using importance sampling to estimate the partition function) and (b) optimizing a policy πθ in the unlabeled MDPs using R̃φ and an entropy-regularized objective, with policy updates performed via MDGPS.
datasets: MuJoCo simulated control tasks (obstacle navigation / obstacle height; 2-link reacher / mass; 2-link reacher with vision / target position; half-cheetah jump / wall height)
baselines: RL policy trained only in labeled MDPs (πRL)
Reward regression (policy learned using a reward function fitted with supervised learning)
Oracle policy with access to true reward in all scenarios
limitations: Requires reward supervision in a subset of MDPs (labeled MDPs) — cannot operate without any reward-labelled instances.
Assumes the reward function is the same across MDPs and that labeled and unlabeled MDPs are sampled from the same underlying distribution (not designed for large, systematic domain shift).
Importance-sampling estimate of the partition function can have high variance early in training, requiring inclusion of πRL samples to mitigate.
Experimental evaluation is conducted in simulation (MuJoCo) — real-world robotic evaluation is not presented in this paper.
_summary.topic_area: Reinforcement learning - semi-supervised RL / inverse RL for robotic control
